1:
On Luke 18:1 — “he spake a parable unto them to this end, that men ought always to pray, and not to faint” — the witnesses mark the parable whose meaning is hung at its door.

The Eastern and Latin fathers read it as the remedy for the trials just foretold. Theophylact: “having spoken of the trials and dangers… coming, He adds… their remedy, namely, constant and earnest prayer.” Augustine: “if… with the most unjust judge, the perseverance of the suppliant at length prevailed… how much more confident ought they to feel who cease not to pray to God.” The grammatical-historical commentators note the unusual prefixed key: Ellicott marks “not to faint” (enkakein) as a word of Luke and Paul (Galatians 6:9; 2 Corinthians 4:1), Robertson glossing it “not to give in to evil… turn coward, lose heart.”

The Reformed Calvin: “perseverance in prayer is a rare and difficult attainment… it is an undoubted evidence of our faith, if we are disappointed of our wish, and yet do not lose courage.” The Puritan Henry: the parable supposes “that all God’s people are praying people.”

So the witnesses open the parable of the importunate widow with its lesson stated in advance: men ought always to pray and never to faint. The fathers tie it to the dark days the previous chapter foretold — the coming trials in which the temptation to give up prayer is strongest. The witnesses note the two extremes the chapter’s twin parables guard against (Wesley): this one against faintness and weariness in prayer, the next against self-confidence. The verse thus sets perseverance, not eloquence, as the mark of true prayer: to keep asking, keep knocking, and not lose heart when the answer tarries — for the God who is petitioned is not a reluctant judge but a faithful Father, and the faith that holds on is the faith he rewards.

2:
On Luke 18:2 — “There was in a city a judge, which feared not God, neither regarded man” — the witnesses mark the lawless judge of the parable.

The grammatical-historical commentators read his character. Barnes: “he that has no regard for God can be expected to have none for man” — the two go together. Robertson: a “hard-boiled judge who knew no one as his superior” (the participle, “regarded not,” meaning to reverence or be shamed by one). Ellicott reads it historically, “testifying to the general disorganisation and corruption of justice” under the government of Galilee and Peraea. The Reformed Gill notes the Jewish courts of judicature in every city.

The verse paints the judge in his worst colours, that the argument of the parable may run from the less to the greater. He feared not God — so no higher authority restrained him; nor regarded man — so no shame or pity moved him. A worse judge could hardly be conceived.

So the witnesses set up the dark figure who is the parable’s foil, not its likeness, to God. The judge fears neither God above nor man before him; he is bound by no reverence and softened by no compassion, the very portrait of corrupt and careless power. The witnesses are careful to mark, with Barnes, that “we are not to suppose that the character of God is at all represented by this judge”; the parable argues by contrast. If even such a man could be moved to do justice, how much more the righteous and merciful God who loves to hear his own? The verse thus prepares the great a fortiori: the persistence that wins a verdict from an unjust judge will far more surely prevail with the Father who needs no wearing-down to be willing.

3:
On Luke 18:3 — “And there was a widow in that city; and she came unto him, saying, Avenge me of mine adversary” — the witnesses mark the helpless suppliant.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the widow’s plight and her plea. Barnes: judges were bound to “show peculiar attention to widows” (Isaiah 1:17; Jeremiah 22:3), being “defenseless… commonly poor… liable to be oppressed.” Clarke insists “avenge me” is better “do me justice against… my adversary” — not revenge but vindication. Robertson notes the imperfect “came oft,” repeated coming. Ellicott lists the law’s long care for the widow (Exodus 22:22; Deuteronomy 27:19).

The verse introduces the one who, by every weakness, embodies the praying saint. The widow has no power, no patron, no weapon but her perseverance; against an adversary and before a careless judge, she has only her repeated, urgent cry for justice.

So the witnesses find in the widow the very image of God’s elect in the world. Like her, they are weak and oppressed, with no strength of their own against their adversaries; like her, their one resource is to come, and keep coming, with their plea. The witnesses mark, with Clarke, that her cry is not for vengeance but for justice — the vindication of a righteous cause — lest the parable seem to sanction a vindictive spirit. Her defencelessness is the point: she cannot bribe, threaten, or compel; she can only importune. And the lesson, the witnesses note, is that this very helplessness, pressed in persistent prayer, is what prevails. The praying soul, owning its weakness and crying without ceasing, holds the one key that opens even a hard heart — and far more surely the willing heart of God.

Her cry, Clarke insists, is not for vengeance but for justice, lest the parable seem to sanction a vindictive spirit. Her defencelessness is the point: she cannot bribe or compel, only importune. 4:
On Luke 18:4 — “And he would not for a while: but afterward he said within himself, Though I fear not God, nor regard man” — the witnesses mark the judge’s long refusal and his self-revealing soliloquy.

The grammatical-historical commentators read the delay and the motive. Barnes: “for a while” means “a considerable time”; the judge attends to the case at last not from justice but, as the soliloquy shows, from self-interest. Ellicott: “the judge was callous and dead to pity… the pleadings of the widow were simply an annoyance.” Robertson notes the imperfect “he would not” — “continued refusal.” Clarke: “how many actions which appear good have neither the love of God, nor… our neighbour… but only self-love… for their… motive.”

The verse turns on the judge’s frank self-knowledge. He owns, within himself, that he fears not God nor regards man — that neither principle nor pity governs him; and yet he is about to act, for a reason that has nothing to do with either.

So the witnesses watch the judge expose his own corruption even as he yields. His long refusal “for a while” measures his indifference; the widow’s cause means nothing to him, her tears an irritation. When at last he relents, it is by no conversion to justice but by a calculation of his own ease, as the next verse will declare. The witnesses note the moral Clarke draws: that a deed outwardly right may spring from a wholly selfish root. Yet the very baseness of his motive sharpens the parable’s force: if mere annoyance can wring justice from a man who fears neither God nor man, how certainly will the importunate prayer of the saints prevail with the God who is all justice and all love. The judge’s grudging surrender, born of self-love, becomes the foil to the Father’s glad answer, born of grace.

5:
On Luke 18:5 — “yet because this widow troubleth me, I will avenge her, lest by her continual coming she weary me” — the witnesses mark the importunity that prevails.

The grammatical-historical commentators dwell on the vivid verb. Clarke: “she weary me” (hypopiaze) means “stun me — a metaphor taken from boxers, who… blacken the eyes” (1 Corinthians 9:27). Ellicott: it “expresses the act of the pugilist who strikes a blow which leaves a livid bruise.” Robertson: “lest she wear me out… because of the furnishing me trouble.” Gill paints her “loud cries and earnest entreaties… floods of tears.”

The verse gives the judge’s reason for relenting: not right, but relief. The widow’s ceaseless coming has become an unbearable trouble; to be rid of her he will do the justice he never cared to do. Her perseverance, not her cause, has conquered him.

So the witnesses fasten on the parable’s point: importunity wins. The widow gains her suit not by power or merit but by refusing to be put off — by a persistence so dogged that the judge, to spare himself, grants her plea. The witnesses note the strikingly homely image, the suppliant who “bruises” the judge with her unrelenting appeals, as a boxer wears down a foe. If such pertinacity can move a heartless man against his own indifference, the Lord argues, how much more shall the persevering prayer of the saints prevail with a God who is not wearied but pleased by their coming. The verse thus crowns the lesson of the parable: the prayer that holds on, that will not be silenced or discouraged, is the prayer that obtains — and the God of the elect, unlike the judge, delights to answer.

The widow gains her suit not by power or merit but by refusing to be put off, with a persistence so dogged the judge grants her plea to spare himself. 6:
On Luke 18:6 — “And the Lord said, Hear what the unjust judge saith” — the witnesses mark the Lord’s own application beginning.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the idiom and the turn. Ellicott and Robertson: “the unjust judge” is literally “the judge of unrighteousness” — the Hebraic characterising genitive, as with “the steward of unrighteousness” in Luke 16:8. Barnes: “give attention to this, and derive… practical instruction.” Clarke supplies the reasoning the Lord intends: “if a person of such an infamous character… could yield… how much more ready must God be, who is infinitely good and merciful.”

The verse marks the hinge from parable to lesson. The Lord bids his hearers attend to the unjust judge’s own words — the grudging “I will avenge her” — for in them lies the whole force of the argument he is about to draw.

So the witnesses see the Lord himself open the meaning. “Hear what the unjust judge saith”: mark how even this man, fearing neither God nor man, was at last compelled to do justice by sheer importunity. The witnesses note the deliberate naming — “the judge of unrighteousness” — which keeps before us that the parable works by contrast: God is the very opposite of this judge, and the comparison is from the worse to the better. If the words of a corrupt judge yield such comfort, how much more the character of God. The verse thus calls the praying soul to listen and learn: the lesson is not that God is reluctant like the judge, but that the persistence which conquers reluctance will far more surely move the willing mercy of the Father who loves to hear.

So the witnesses see the Lord himself open the meaning. “Hear what the unjust judge saith”: mark how even this man, fearing neither God nor man, was compelled to do justice by sheer importunity. The witnesses note the deliberate naming, “the judge of unrighteousness,” which keeps before us that the parable works by contrast: God is the very opposite of this judge. The lesson is not that God is reluctant, but that the persistence which conquers reluctance will far more surely move the willing mercy of the Father who loves to hear.
7:
On Luke 18:7 — “And shall not God avenge his own elect, which cry day and night unto him, though he bear long with them?” — the witnesses mark the great a fortiori of the parable.

The grammatical-historical commentators draw the contrast. Barnes: “we are not to suppose that the character of God is… represented by this judge… many circumstances of a parable are not to be interpreted closely.” Robertson: “God delays… not through indifference, but through patient forbearance” — “and he is longsuffering” over them. Lightfoot ties “bear long with them” to 2 Peter 3:9, God’s patience toward his elect before Jerusalem’s fall. The Reformed Gill expounds the “elect” whom God will surely vindicate.

The verse turns the parable to its point. If a wicked judge yields to importunity, shall not the righteous God answer his own chosen, who cry to him without ceasing? The seeming delay is no neglect but a long-suffering patience; the answer is certain, however it tarry.

So the witnesses read the heart of the comfort. God’s elect, like the widow, cry day and night; and though God “bears long” with them, his delay is utterly unlike the judge’s indifference — it is the patience of a Father who has his own wise season. The witnesses are careful to mark the contrast that governs the whole: the judge had to be worn down against his will; God needs no wearing-down, for he loves his own and longs to help them. The “bearing long” is part of his mercy — toward his persecutors, that they may repent; toward his elect, that their faith may be proved and refined. The verse thus assures the praying soul that the delay it feels is not God’s reluctance but his forbearance, and that the cry which seems unheard is heard, and shall be answered in its time.

So the witnesses read the heart of the comfort. God’s elect, like the widow, cry day and night; and though God bears long with them, his delay is utterly unlike the judge’s indifference — it is the patience of a Father with his own wise season. The delay the praying soul feels is not reluctance but forbearance, and the cry that seems unheard is heard, and shall be answered in its time.
8:
On Luke 18:8 — “I tell you that he will avenge them speedily. Nevertheless when the Son of man cometh, shall he find faith on the earth?” — the witnesses mark the promise of swift help and the searching closing question.

The grammatical-historical commentators weigh both clauses. Barnes: “speedily” means “suddenly, unexpectedly… perhaps when they were nearly ready to… sink into despair.” On the question, Ellicott: it “implies… an answer in the negative,” for “iniquity was abounding, and the love of many waxing cold” (James 5:8). Robertson notes ara expecting a negative answer; Clarke renders it “shall he find fidelity” in the land. Lightfoot and Gill tie the “coming” first to the vengeance on Jerusalem.

The Reformed Calvin draws the warning: men “sink under their calamities… because they neglect the true remedy” of prayer; and Christ asks the question to forearm us against the offence of seeing “all things in shameful confusion.”

So the witnesses join a bright promise to a sober question. God will avenge his elect “speedily” — not always soon by our reckoning, but suddenly and surely when his hour strikes, often at the point of their deepest despair. Yet the Lord ends not with comfort but with a question that searches the praying Church: when he comes, will he find faith — the persevering, importunate faith the parable commends — still alive on the earth? The witnesses note the implied negative: faith will be rare, the love of many cold, the temptation to faint in prayer widely yielded to. The verse thus turns the whole parable into an exhortation: the danger is not that God will fail to answer, but that men will fail to keep praying; and the question hangs over every generation, whether it will hold fast the faith that cries day and night until he comes.

So the witnesses join a bright promise to a sober question. God will avenge his elect “speedily” — suddenly and surely when his hour strikes, often at the point of deepest despair. Yet the Lord ends not with comfort but with a searching question: when he comes, will he find faith — the persevering faith the parable commends — alive on the earth? The implied answer is negative: faith will be rare, love cold, the temptation to faint widely yielded to. The danger is not that God will fail to answer, but that men will fail to keep praying.
9:
On Luke 18:9 — “And he spake this parable unto certain which trusted in themselves that they were righteous, and despised others” — the witnesses mark the target of the parable of the Pharisee and the publican.

The Eastern and Latin fathers read it as a lesson against pride. Augustine: “since faith is not a gift of the proud but of the humble, our Lord proceeds to add a parable concerning humility and against pride.” Theophylact: pride “beyond all other passions disturbs the mind of man… it is… a contempt of God.” The grammatical-historical commentators note the two faults named: Barnes, they “trusted in themselves… to their own works”; Robertson, “set all others at naught” (exoutheneo, treat as nothing). The Puritan Henry: they had “a great conceit of themselves… and a great contempt of others.”

The Reformed Calvin: “no disease is more dangerous than arrogance… fixed in the marrow of” our bones; believers must come before God “with humility and abasement.”

So the witnesses see the parable aimed squarely at self-righteousness and the contempt that breeds. Its hearers were those who trusted their own goodness for acceptance with God and despised all who fell short of their standard — two sins, the witnesses note, that grow from one root: pride. The fathers mark that this pride is the deadly foe of prayer and faith, a very contempt of God, for it ascribes to self what is God’s gift. Wesley adds the sharp point that the Pharisee “was no hypocrite” but “sincerely trusted in himself” — the parable strikes not at pretended but at real and confident self-righteousness. The verse thus frames the contrast to come, between the proud man who trusts himself and is rejected, and the humble sinner who trusts God’s mercy and goes home justified.

So the witnesses see the parable aimed at self-righteousness and the contempt it breeds — two sins from one root, pride. The fathers mark that this pride is the deadly foe of prayer and faith, a very contempt of God, for it ascribes to self what is God’s gift. Wesley adds the sharp point that the Pharisee “was no hypocrite” but sincerely trusted himself — the parable strikes at real, confident self-righteousness. It frames the contrast to come: the proud man who trusts himself, rejected; the humble sinner who trusts God’s mercy, justified.
10:
On Luke 18:10 — “Two men went up into the temple to pray; the one a Pharisee, and the other a publican” — the witnesses mark the two worshippers set side by side.

The grammatical-historical commentators sketch the scene. Ellicott: “went up” was “strictly justified by the position of the Temple,” high on Moriah; “the two words” — Pharisee, publican — “would be more pictorially suggestive to the disciples than they are… to us,” the one the pattern of repute, the other of reproach. Gill notes the Jewish esteem for prayer in the temple. The Reformed Calvin: the two “seem to manifest the same ardour of piety, while yet they are exceedingly unlike.”

The verse stages the contrast with deliberate art. Two men go up to the same temple, at the same hour, for the same act of prayer; but one is a Pharisee, esteemed righteous, the other a publican, despised as a sinner. Same place, same posture — opposite hearts.

So the witnesses watch the Lord set the most respected and the most reviled of men together before God, that the true measure of worship may appear. To the eye they are alike, both at prayer in the holy place; but God reads what the eye cannot — the spirit in which each comes. The witnesses note how the parable overturns every outward reckoning: the man the world honoured will be rejected, the man it scorned accepted, and the difference will lie not in the form of their prayer but in the temper of their souls. The verse thus prepares the great reversal of the chapter’s teaching — that what justifies is not the rank or repute of the worshipper but the humility that casts itself on mercy; and it bids every reader ask, not where he stands in men’s esteem, but how he comes before God.

11:
On Luke 18:11 — “The Pharisee stood and prayed thus with himself, God, I thank thee, that I am not as other men are… or even as this publican” — the witnesses dissect the proud man’s prayer.

The Eastern fathers expose its arrogance and contempt. Theophylact notes the order: “he first speaks of that which he had not, and then of that which he had.” Augustine: by “other men” he means “all besides himself” — “I am righteous, the rest are sinners.” Chrysostom: “to despise the whole race of man was not enough… he must yet attack the Publican… To give thanks is not to heap reproaches on others.” The grammatical-historical Robertson: he “struck an attitude ostentatiously… a soliloquy with his own soul, a complacent recital.”

The Reformed Calvin presses the deeper fault: he is blamed not for boasting of free-will but “for trusting that God was reconciled to him by the merits of his works” — a thanksgiving that was really self-praise.

So the witnesses anatomise the Pharisee’s prayer as a monument of pride. It begins with God’s name but ends in self; its thanks are not for grace received but for fancied superiority — that he is not as other, lesser men. The witnesses mark the contempt that crowns it: not content to exalt himself, he must trample the publican standing near, turning prayer into an accusation of his neighbour. Chrysostom’s rebuke is keen — true thanksgiving does not reproach others. And Calvin names the root: the man trusted his own works for acceptance, so that even his “God, I thank thee” was a hymn to himself. The verse thus shows the prayer that does not justify: a self-satisfied recital that asks nothing, confesses nothing, and despises a brother — the very pattern of the pride that bars the soul from grace.

So the witnesses anatomise the Pharisee’s prayer as a monument of pride. It begins with God’s name but ends in self; its thanks are not for grace received but for fancied superiority. The contempt that crowns it — trampling the publican near him — turns prayer into an accusation of his neighbour; Chrysostom’s rebuke is keen, that true thanksgiving does not reproach others. And Calvin names the root: he trusted his works for acceptance, so that even his “God, I thank thee” was a hymn to himself — the prayer that asks nothing, confesses nothing, and does not justify.
12:
On Luke 18:12 — “I fast twice in the week, I give tithes of all that I possess” — the witnesses mark the Pharisee’s recital of his merits.

The grammatical-historical commentators detail the works. Robertson: the law required one fast a year (Leviticus 16:29); the Pharisees “added… twice a week.” Barnes: they “made more a matter of merit of it because it was voluntary.” Clarke notes the present tense, “all that I acquire” — he tithed his income as fast as he gained it. Lightfoot supplies the rabbinic background of public and private fasts. The Reformed Calvin: this is to say “he performed more than the law required,” like “Popish monks” boasting of works of supererogation.

The verse fills out the prayer’s second half — the positive catalogue of the Pharisee’s virtues. Having distanced himself from other men’s sins, he now parades his own observances: a fasting beyond the law’s demand, a scrupulous tithing of all he has. His prayer asks for nothing; it only displays.

So the witnesses read the Pharisee’s works as the ground of a false confidence. He fasts twice weekly where the law asked but once a year, and tithes with exact care — doing, as Wesley sums, “all the good I can” and “no harm.” The witnesses do not deny the deeds; the fault is not in fasting or tithing but in resting on them for acceptance, making them a credit by which God should be in his debt. Calvin marks the spirit of supererogation — the boast of doing more than is required, as if one could lay God under obligation. The verse thus completes the portrait of self-righteous prayer: a man counting up his religious performances before God, secure in his own merit, and so blind to his need of the mercy the publican will beg. His works, real as they are, become the very screen that hides his sin from himself.

The witnesses do not deny the deeds; the fault is resting on them for acceptance, making them a credit by which God should be in his debt. 13:
On Luke 18:13 — “And the publican, standing afar off, would not lift up so much as his eyes unto heaven, but smote upon his breast, saying, God be merciful to me a sinner” — the witnesses mark the prayer of true humility.

The grammatical-historical commentators read every token of contrition. Barnes and Robertson: “afar off” not from the sanctuary but from the crowd and the Pharisee, with “no ostentation”; “would not lift” (double negative) — refused even to raise his eyes. Clarke lists the signs: the downcast eyes of “deep distress,” the smitten breast of self-reproach. Ellicott: he kept “away… from the devout and respectable Pharisee,” like “some forlorn and desperate castaway.”

The Reformed Calvin: Christ “merely describes the tokens of humility… humility lies in not refusing to acknowledge our sins, but condemning ourselves, and thus anticipating the judgment of God.”

So the witnesses gather in the publican’s prayer the very opposite of the Pharisee’s. He stands afar off, ashamed to press near; he dares not lift his eyes; he smites his breast in sorrow; and his whole prayer is one cry, “God be merciful to me a sinner.” He brings no merits, parades no works, despises no neighbour; he owns himself the sinner and casts himself wholly on mercy. The witnesses note how every posture preaches humility — the distance, the lowered eyes, the stricken breast — not as a prescribed ritual (Calvin), but as the spontaneous language of a broken heart. The verse thus sets before us the prayer that justifies: short, lowly, empty of self, full of confession, asking only for the mercy of God. Where the Pharisee told God his goodness, the publican tells God his sin — and it is he who goes home accepted.

So the witnesses gather in the publican’s prayer the very opposite of the Pharisee’s. He stands afar off, dares not lift his eyes, smites his breast, and cries only, “God be merciful to me a sinner.” He brings no merits, parades no works, despises no neighbour; he owns himself the sinner and casts himself wholly on mercy. 14:
On Luke 18:14 — “I tell you, this man went down to his house justified rather than the other: for every one that exalteth himself shall be abased” — the witnesses mark the verdict and its law.

The grammatical-historical commentators stress the word “justified.” Barnes: it means “accepted or approved of God… to declare or treat as righteous” — the publican’s prayer accepted, the Pharisee’s not. Robertson notes the perfect participle and that this maxim was already used at Luke 14:11. Ellicott: the perfect implies “a completed and abiding justification,” and the “house” is the test — “home-life is the test of the reality… of our worship.” The Reformed Calvin: “to justify… means to stand before God as if we were righteous,” the publican accepted, the Pharisee “totally rejected.”

The verse delivers the parable’s startling judgement: the despised publican, not the honoured Pharisee, went home justified. And it grounds the verdict in the great law of the kingdom — self-exaltation ends in abasement, self-humbling in exaltation.

So the witnesses read the reversal that crowns the parable. God’s reckoning overturns man’s: the sinner who pleaded mercy is declared righteous, while the saint who pleaded his merits is sent empty away. The witnesses, with Bede, see in it the whole gospel — the proud Pharisee a figure of those who trust the law’s righteousness and go down humbled, the lowly publican of those who confess their sin and are exalted. They mark the doctrinal weight of “justified”: to be accounted righteous before God, not by works but by casting oneself on mercy. And they note that the closing maxim binds this parable to the chapter’s whole theme of humility, from the little children to the rich ruler. The verse thus teaches that the way to be lifted up is to bow down: God justifies the humble who own their sin, and abases the proud who trust themselves.

Bede sees in it the whole gospel — the proud going down humbled, the lowly exalted. The witnesses mark the doctrinal weight of “justified”: accounted righteous before God, not by works but by casting oneself on mercy. 15:
On Luke 18:15 — “And they brought unto him also infants, that he would touch them: but when his disciples saw it, they rebuked them” — the witnesses mark the children brought to Christ.

The Eastern and Latin fathers read it as a lesson in humility following the parable. Theophylact: “our Lord teaches us a lesson of humility by His own example; He does not turn away the little children… but graciously receives them.” Augustine: to whom are they brought “but to the Saviour… to be saved.” Ambrose excuses the disciples — they rebuked the parents “not from envy… but” from love, guarding their Master. The grammatical-historical Robertson notes Luke’s word “babes” (brephe, infants), stronger than Mark’s and Matthew’s “little children” (Mark 10:13; Matthew 19:13).

The Puritan Henry marks the fitness of the placing: this story “very fitly follows here after… the publican, as a confirmation” that the humble are accepted, “and for them Christ has blessings in store.”

So the witnesses see the bringing of the infants as the living illustration of the humility just taught. Where the disciples, still thinking such little ones beneath their Master’s notice, rebuked the parents, the fathers find in the very act of bringing them a parable of faith: the helpless are brought to the Saviour to be blessed. The witnesses note Luke’s precise word — not merely children but babes in arms — underscoring that even the least and most dependent are not turned away. The placing, Henry observes, is deliberate: hard on the heels of the publican who was justified by humble faith comes the picture of infants received by grace, that we may see in them the very temper the kingdom requires. The verse thus prepares the great saying to follow — that the kingdom belongs to such, and must be received as a little child.

Where the disciples rebuked the parents, thinking such little ones beneath their Master, the fathers find in the very act a parable of faith: the helpless are brought to the Saviour to be blessed. 16:
On Luke 18:16 — “But Jesus called them unto him, and said, Suffer little children to come unto me, and forbid them not: for of such is the kingdom of God” — the witnesses mark the Lord’s welcome and his word.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the rebuke reversed. Robertson: Jesus “called the children with their parents to himself and then rebuked the disciples for their rebuke”; the plea that children “be allowed to come to him” is precisely Mark’s (Mark 10:14). Ellicott marks the close agreement with Mark, likely from Luke’s personal communication with him. Wesley reads “of such is the kingdom”: “such are subjects of the Messiah’s kingdom… and such as these it properly belongs to.”

The verse overturns the disciples’ misjudgement. Far from too lowly for his notice, the children are called to him with an express command that none forbid them; and the reason is the weightiest — the kingdom of God belongs to such as these.

So the witnesses see the Lord both welcome the little ones and rebuke the disciples who would have kept them off. He will not have the helpless hindered from coming to him; he claims them, blesses them, and declares the kingdom theirs — or rather the kingdom of those who are like them. The witnesses note the double force of “of such”: it embraces both the children themselves and all who share the childlike spirit of humble dependence. Where the disciples measured worth by importance, the Lord measures it by lowliness, and so opens his arms to the least. The verse thus rebukes every pride that despises the small and the simple, and holds up the child — trusting, dependent, unpretending — as the very pattern of those to whom the kingdom is given; preparing the next verse’s solemn condition.

So the witnesses see the Lord both welcome the little ones and rebuke the disciples who would keep them off. He will not have the helpless hindered, but claims them, blesses them, and declares the kingdom theirs — or of those who are like them. The witnesses note the double force of “of such”: it embraces both the children and all who share their spirit of humble dependence. Where the disciples measured worth by importance, the Lord measures it by lowliness, and so opens his arms to the least — rebuking every pride that despises the small and simple.
17:
On Luke 18:17 — “Whosoever shall not receive the kingdom of God as a little child shall in no wise enter therein” — the witnesses mark the childlike condition of entrance.

The voices here are brief but pointed. The grammatical-historical Robertson: “Jesus makes the child the model for those who seek entrance… not the adult the model for the child… He does not say that the child is already in the kingdom without coming to him.” Barnes refers to the like teaching (Mark 10:15; 1 Peter 1:14). Gill notes the Lord takes occasion to teach “humility… and guard against pride and vanity.”

The verse lays down the condition with the strongest negative: none shall enter the kingdom who does not receive it as a little child. The childlike temper is not an ornament of discipleship but the very gate; without it, entrance is barred.

So the witnesses read the saying as the heart of the chapter’s teaching on humility. To receive the kingdom “as a little child” is to receive it as the child receives — with simple trust, without claim or merit, owning utter dependence and gladly taking what is freely given. The witnesses note Robertson’s precision: the child is the model not because childhood is innocence but because the child believes, depends, and does not boast. This is the very opposite of the Pharisee’s self-trust and the rich ruler’s self-confidence that the chapter sets against it. The verse thus binds together the publican who pleaded mercy, the infants Christ blessed, and the warning to the proud: the kingdom is entered only by those who come empty-handed and lowly, taking it as a gift; and the self-sufficient, however rich or righteous, shall in no wise enter in.

So the witnesses read the saying as the heart of the chapter’s teaching on humility. To receive the kingdom “as a little child” is to receive it with simple trust, without claim or merit, owning utter dependence and gladly taking what is freely given — the very opposite of the Pharisee’s self-trust and the ruler’s self-confidence. The witnesses note Robertson’s precision: the child is the model because it believes, depends, and does not boast. The kingdom is entered only by those who come empty-handed and lowly; the self-sufficient, however rich or righteous, shall in no wise enter in.
18:
On Luke 18:18 — “And a certain ruler asked him, saying, Good Master, what shall I do to inherit eternal life?” — the witnesses mark the rich ruler’s earnest question.

The Eastern and Latin fathers probe the question and the man. Bede: having heard that only the childlike enter, the ruler “entreats Him to explain… openly by what works he may merit… eternal life.” Ambrose: in saying “Good Master, he ought to have said, Good God.” Chrysostom is willing “to call this ruler covetous… but… not… a tempter.” The grammatical-historical Robertson: “By doing what shall I inherit?” — the same question as the lawyer’s (Luke 10:25); “this young man probably thought that by some one act he could obtain eternal life.”

The Puritan Henry: “it is a blessed sight to see persons of distinction… distinguish themselves… by their concern about their souls and another life.”

So the witnesses read the ruler’s coming as sincere but flawed. He is earnest — a man of rank and wealth running to ask the way to eternal life, which Henry counts a hopeful sign; yet his very question betrays his error, for he asks what he must do, supposing eternal life a prize to be earned by some great deed. The fathers note a further fault in his address: he calls Christ “good” while thinking him only a teacher, when goodness in the full sense belongs to God. The witnesses thus set the ruler beside the publican and the children: where they came empty and dependent, he comes confident of his own ability to perform whatever is required. His question opens the great encounter in which the Lord will lay bare the one thing his self-confidence cannot supply — and so test whether he will receive the kingdom as a little child.

So the witnesses read the ruler’s coming as sincere but flawed. He is earnest — a man of rank running to ask the way to eternal life, which Henry counts hopeful; yet his question betrays his error, asking what he must do, as though eternal life were a prize earned by some great deed. The fathers note a further fault: he calls Christ “good” while thinking him only a teacher, when goodness in full belongs to God. Where the publican and the children came empty and dependent, he comes confident of his own ability — ripe for the test that will lay bare the one thing he lacks.
19:
On Luke 18:19 — “And Jesus said unto him, Why callest thou me good? none is good, save one, that is, God” — the witnesses weigh the Lord’s probing reply.

The voices here are few. The grammatical-historical Lightfoot argues that the ruler “acknowledged Jesus for the true Messiah,” as others did who were not yet his disciples; the question is meant to lead him from a careless compliment to a true confession. Ellicott notes “the agreement with St. Mark is again closer than with St. Matthew” (Mark 10:18). Gill takes the Lord as beginning his answer from “the character he gave him.”

The verse turns the man’s own word back on him. He had called Christ “good” lightly, as a flattering address to a teacher; the Lord presses him — why use a word that belongs, in its fullness, to God alone? The question is not a denial of Christ’s goodness but a challenge to think what he is saying.

So the witnesses read the Lord’s reply as a probe of the ruler’s heart, not a disclaimer of his own deity. He does not say he is not good; he asks why the man calls him good, that the ruler may weigh his words and be led to a deeper truth. If goodness in the highest sense is God’s alone, then to call Jesus “good” truly is to confess more than the ruler yet understood. The witnesses, with Lightfoot, see the question as evangelical, designed to lift the man from a conventional courtesy toward a real recognition of who stands before him. It also pricks his confident question: if none is good but God, then the ruler’s assumption that he can simply “do” some good thing to earn life is already undercut. The verse thus opens the way for the searching test to follow, sifting compliment from faith.

So the witnesses read the Lord’s reply as a probe of the ruler’s heart, not a disclaimer of his deity. He does not say he is not good; he asks why the man calls him good, that the ruler may weigh his words and be led to a deeper truth. 20:
On Luke 18:20 — “Thou knowest the commandments, Do not commit adultery, Do not kill… Honour thy father and thy mother” — the witnesses mark the Lord pointing to the law.

The Eastern and Latin fathers read the order and the point. Titus of Bostra: “Know God first, and then will it be time to seek what you ask” — the Lord sends him to the commandments. Theophylact notes the placing of “Honour thy father and mother” last; Ambrose: honour is “not only… paying respect, but also… giving bountifully… Feed your father, feed your mother.” The grammatical-historical commentators note the second-table commandments cited (Exodus 20:12; Romans 13:9), and that Luke, with Matthew, omits Mark’s “defraud not” (Ellicott).

The verse meets the ruler’s “what shall I do” on his own ground. He asked for a work; the Lord refers him to the commandments he already knows — the plain duties of the second table, love of neighbour, that the law has long required.

So the witnesses see the Lord lead the ruler to the law, not as the final answer but as the mirror that will show his need. Since the man trusts in doing, he is sent to what God commands — the familiar precepts against adultery, murder, theft, false witness, and for honouring parents. The witnesses note that Christ cites the second table, the duties toward men, where the ruler’s self-satisfaction is most easily exposed. The fathers find depth even here: that honouring parents includes provision, not mere respect. The Lord’s aim, the witnesses agree, is not to teach salvation by law-keeping but to take the ruler at his word and bring him, through the law, to discover the one thing he lacks. The verse thus sets the stage for the test: confronted with the commandments, the ruler will claim to have kept them all — and so reveal how little he knows his own heart.

Since he trusts in doing, he is sent to what God commands — the familiar precepts of the second table, where his self-satisfaction is most easily exposed. 21:
On Luke 18:21 — “And he said, All these have I kept from my youth up” — the witnesses mark the ruler’s confident claim.

The voices here are brief. The grammatical-historical Ellicott notes the detail “from my youth up” as a point Luke shares with Mark (Mark 10:20), as also the omission of Matthew’s further question, “What lack I yet?” Gill takes the ruler as answering “to Jesus” that he had kept all these commandments to that very time.

The verse gives the ruler’s answer in all its untroubled assurance. He claims to have kept every one of these commandments, and from his earliest years — a clear conscience, or so he supposes, toward the whole second table of the law.

So the witnesses read the claim as the revealing of the ruler’s self-righteousness, however sincere. He believes himself to have kept the law from childhood, and says so without hesitation — a man, like the Pharisee of the parable, confident in his own goodness. The witnesses note the irony that he should think the commandments so easily mastered, when their spiritual depth (as the Lord taught in the Sermon on the Mount) reaches the heart and condemns all. His answer is not hypocrisy but blindness; he measures himself by the outward letter and finds nothing wanting. The verse thus brings him to the very edge of self-discovery: having professed a perfect keeping, he is ripe for the one demand that will test whether his heart is as free as he imagines — and expose the idol his outward obedience has hidden.

So the witnesses read the claim as the revealing of the ruler’s self-righteousness, however sincere. He believes he has kept every commandment from childhood, and says so without hesitation — like the Pharisee, confident in his own goodness. The witnesses note the irony that he should think the commandments so easily mastered, when their spiritual depth reaches the heart and condemns all. His answer is not hypocrisy but blindness; he measures himself by the outward letter and finds nothing wanting — ripe for the one demand that will expose the idol his obedience has hidden.
22:
On Luke 18:22 — “Yet lackest thou one thing: sell all that thou hast, and distribute unto the poor… and come, follow me” — the witnesses mark the one decisive test.

The grammatical-historical commentators read the searching demand. Robertson: “one thing still fails thee” — “an amazing compliment for one who was aiming at perfection,” the youth sincere in his claim. Ellicott notes Luke, with Matthew, omits Mark’s “taking up thy cross.” The Wesleyan-Arminian Wesley gives the key: the one thing was “to love God more than mammon… to cure his love of the world… Christ commanded him to sell all… But he does not command us to do this.” Barnes points to “treasure in heaven” (Matthew 6:19–20).

The verse lays bare the ruler’s lack with a single command. For all his law-keeping, one thing fails — and the Lord names it by the test that will reveal it: let him sell all, give to the poor, and follow Christ. The demand probes precisely where his heart is bound.

So the witnesses read the command not as a universal rule but as a heart-searching diagnosis. The ruler had kept the outward law, yet one thing was wanting — he loved his wealth more than God; and Christ, the reader of hearts, prescribes the very cure that exposes the disease. The witnesses, with Wesley, are careful: this is not a law for all to sell everything, but the particular remedy for a particular idol; what is required of all is to love God above mammon, holding all things loosely. The call to “follow me” shows the true end — not poverty for its own sake, but Christ in the place his riches held. The verse thus brings the self-confident ruler to the one demand his self-confidence cannot meet, offering him treasure in heaven for the treasure on earth he will not surrender — and so testing whether he will receive the kingdom as a little child.

So the witnesses read the command not as a universal rule but as a heart-searching diagnosis. The ruler had kept the outward law, yet one thing was wanting — he loved his wealth more than God; and Christ prescribes the very cure that exposes the disease. The witnesses, with Wesley, are careful: this is not a law for all to sell everything, but the particular remedy for a particular idol; what is required of all is to love God above mammon. The call to “follow me” shows the true end — not poverty for its own sake, but Christ in the place his riches held.
23:
On Luke 18:23 — “And when he heard this, he was very sorrowful: for he was very rich” — the witnesses mark the ruler’s grief and its cause.

The voices here are brief. The grammatical-historical Ellicott: Luke’s word for sorrow “stands half-way between St. Matthew’s ‘sorrowing’ and St. Mark’s vivid ‘lowering’ or ‘frowning’” (Mark 10:22); “very rich” is Luke’s equivalent for “he had great possessions.” Robertson: “exceedingly sorrowful… very rich… today, a multimillionaire.”

The verse records the test’s outcome in the ruler’s face. The one command he could not meet fills him with sorrow; and the Evangelist names the reason plainly — he was very rich. His wealth, which he could not bear to lose, becomes the measure of his grief.

So the witnesses read the ruler’s sorrow as the verdict of the test. The demand has found him out: he loved his riches more than the eternal life he sought, and the choice between them grieves him to the soul. The witnesses note the sad self-disclosure — the man who thought he had kept all the commandments now discovers, by his own sorrow, that the first commandment was broken, his heart given to mammon rather than God. His grief is real, but it is the grief of a man who will not pay the price, not the godly sorrow that leads to repentance. The very greatness of his wealth, which the world counted his blessing, is here named as the cause of his loss. The verse thus shows the tragedy of a soul near the kingdom yet held back by what it will not surrender — sorrowful, but unsaved, because too rich to follow.

So the witnesses read the ruler’s sorrow as the verdict of the test. The demand has found him out: he loved his riches more than the eternal life he sought, and the choice grieves him to the soul. The witnesses note the sad self-disclosure — the man who thought he had kept all the commandments now discovers, by his own sorrow, that the first was broken, his heart given to mammon. His grief is real, but it is the grief of one who will not pay the price. The verse shows the tragedy of a soul near the kingdom yet held back by what it will not surrender.
24:
On Luke 18:24 — “And when Jesus saw that he was very sorrowful, he said, How hardly shall they that have riches enter into the kingdom of God!” — the witnesses mark the Lord’s lament over riches.

The Eastern and Latin fathers weigh the word “hardly.” Theophylact: “He says not, It is impossible… but it is difficult. For they might through their riches reap a heavenly reward, but it is a hard thing” — the difficulty lying in the grip of wealth on the heart. Bede draws from the sequel that he who “has despised all earthly affections… shall receive far greater.” The grammatical-historical Barnes points to the peril of riches (1 Timothy 6:9); Robertson reads the futuristic present, “shall they enter.”

The verse turns the ruler’s sad example into a general warning. The Lord, seeing the man’s grief, marks how hard it is for the rich to enter the kingdom — not that wealth itself shuts them out, but that it so easily masters the heart and holds it back.

So the witnesses read the saying as a sober commentary on what they have just witnessed. The rich ruler’s sorrow proves the point: riches make entrance into the kingdom hard, for they bind the affections to earth and resist the surrender the kingdom requires. The witnesses are careful, with Theophylact, to mark the precise word — “hardly,” not “impossibly” — for the rich are not hopeless; some, like Abraham or Zacchaeus, held wealth and yet followed God. The danger is in the heart’s clinging, the trust and love that money breeds, which the next verses will declare can be overcome only by the power of God. The verse thus universalises the ruler’s case into a warning for every age: that prosperity, far from being the simple blessing men suppose, is a grave spiritual peril, hard to carry without being held by it.

So the witnesses read the saying as a sober commentary on what they have just witnessed. The rich ruler’s sorrow proves the point: riches make entrance hard, for they bind the affections to earth and resist the surrender the kingdom requires. The danger is in the heart’s clinging, the trust and love that money breeds — a grave spiritual peril, hard to carry without being held by it.
25:
On Luke 18:25 — “For it is easier for a camel to go through a needle’s eye, than for a rich man to enter into the kingdom of God” — the witnesses mark the vivid proverb of impossibility.

The grammatical-historical commentators handle the image and a variant. Robertson: “a needle’s eye… probably a current proverb for the impossible,” Luke’s word for “needle” (belone, a surgeon’s needle) being a more classical term than Matthew’s and Mark’s — “a sign of Luke’s culture” (Ellicott). Clarke notes some manuscripts read “a cable” (kamilon) for “camel” (kamelon) (Matthew 19:24), though the camel is the stronger and likely reading.

The verse heightens the difficulty into a picture of sheer impossibility. A camel through a needle’s eye — the largest beast through the smallest opening — is the Lord’s proverb for what cannot, of itself, be done: so hard, humanly, is the entrance of a rich man into the kingdom.

So the witnesses read the saying as the climax of the warning on riches, deliberately extravagant to startle. The Lord does not soften “hardly” but sharpens it to a proverb of impossibility, that the disciples may feel the full peril of wealth. The witnesses note the medical precision of Luke’s word for needle — the physician’s touch — and dismiss as needless the reading “cable” for “camel,” for the very point is the grotesque impossibility. The image is not a literal gate or a tame metaphor but a humanly hopeless thing, as the disciples’ dismay in the next verse confirms. Yet the impossibility is, as the Lord will say, the human impossibility only; what no man can do, God can. The verse thus drives the rich, and all who trust in riches, to despair of self and look to the power of God, who alone can loose the heart’s grip on gold and bring a rich man through the needle’s eye.

So the witnesses read the saying as the climax of the warning on riches, deliberately extravagant to startle. The Lord does not soften “hardly” but sharpens it to a proverb of impossibility, that the disciples may feel the full peril of wealth. The witnesses note the medical precision of Luke’s word for needle and dismiss as needless the reading “cable” for “camel,” for the very point is the grotesque impossibility. Yet it is the human impossibility only; what no man can do, God can — driving the rich to despair of self and look to the power of God.
26:
On Luke 18:26 — “And they that heard it said, Who then can be saved?” — the witnesses mark the hearers’ dismay.

The voices here are brief. The grammatical-historical Robertson catches the assumption behind the question: “wealth was assumed to be mark of divine favour, not a hindrance to salvation” — so if the rich could scarcely be saved, who could? Ellicott notes Luke’s phrasing (“they that heard”) may suggest “others may have been present besides the disciples.” Gill identifies the speakers as the disciples.

The verse registers the shock the saying produced. If it is harder than a camel through a needle’s eye for a rich man to be saved, the hearers reason, then salvation seems beyond anyone’s reach — “Who then can be saved?”

So the witnesses read the question as the measure of how startling the Lord’s words were. The disciples shared the common Jewish view that riches were a token of God’s favour and the rich the likeliest to be saved; to hear instead that wealth was a grave obstacle overturned their whole reckoning. If even the favoured rich could hardly enter, what hope for any? The witnesses note that the dismay was salutary: it stripped away the false confidence that tied salvation to outward prosperity, and prepared them to hear that salvation is, for rich and poor alike, beyond human power. The question “Who then can be saved?” is, rightly answered, the beginning of grace — for it drives the soul from every reliance on self or circumstance to the one source of saving power, which the Lord will name in the next verse: with God.
