The disciples shared the common view that riches were a token of God’s favour and the rich the likeliest to be saved; to hear that wealth was a grave obstacle overturned their whole reckoning. If even the favoured rich could hardly enter, what hope for any? The witnesses note the dismay was salutary: it stripped away the false confidence that tied salvation to prosperity, and prepared them to hear that salvation, for rich and poor alike, is beyond human power — the beginning of grace.
27:
On Luke 18:27 — “And he said, The things which are impossible with men are possible with God” — the witnesses mark the answer that turns despair to hope.

The grammatical-historical commentators read the saving paradox. Robertson: “take your stand beside God and the impossible becomes possible… God can break the grip of gold” — confirming that the camel-saying meant the humanly impossible. Barnes and Ellicott set it among the great affirmations of divine power (Jeremiah 32:17; Luke 1:37), “a generalised axiom” that what man cannot do, God can.

The verse answers the disciples’ despairing question with the one ground of all hope. Salvation is indeed impossible to man — the rich cannot save themselves, nor can any — but it is possible with God, whose power can do what human ability cannot.

So the witnesses read the Lord’s reply as the heart of the gospel of grace. Having driven the hearers to ask “Who then can be saved?”, he does not lower the demand but lifts their eyes from man’s impossibility to God’s power. The witnesses note the precise comfort: the Lord grants that, humanly, salvation is hopeless — for rich and poor alike no man can change his own heart or earn eternal life — but with God it is possible, for he can break the grip of gold, soften the hardest heart, and bring even a camel through the needle’s eye. The verse thus completes the lesson of the rich ruler: not that the rich are doomed, but that salvation, for them and for all, is wholly the work of God. It throws the soul off every self-confidence — the Pharisee’s, the ruler’s, our own — onto the almighty grace that alone can save.

So the witnesses read the Lord’s reply as the heart of the gospel of grace. Having driven the hearers to ask “Who then can be saved?”, he does not lower the demand but lifts their eyes from man’s impossibility to God’s power. The Lord grants that, humanly, salvation is hopeless — no man can change his own heart or earn eternal life — but with God it is possible, for he can break the grip of gold and soften the hardest heart. The verse throws the soul off every self-confidence onto the almighty grace that alone can save.
28:
On Luke 18:28 — “Then Peter said, Lo, we have left all, and followed thee” — the witnesses mark Peter’s response.

The voices here are brief. The grammatical-historical Robertson reads Peter not boasting but reacting: “not in a spirit of boastfulness, but rather… from their consternation at what has happened.” He glosses “our own” (ta idia) as “home, business… right here is where so many fail.” Clarke: “our trades, our houses, and families” (Matthew 19:27). Gill notes Peter was “always the most forward to speak.”

The verse sets Peter and his fellows against the rich ruler. Where the ruler would not leave his wealth, they have left all — their homes, trades, families — to follow Christ; and Peter, prompted by the scene, ventures to name it.

So the witnesses read Peter’s word as a question more than a boast, born of the disciples’ consternation. They have just seen a rich man turn away rather than forsake his goods, and heard how hard salvation is; Peter, by contrast, points to what they have actually done — left their own to follow the Lord — and implicitly asks what shall come of it. The witnesses note the human touch: the disciples, far from wealthy, had yet given up their little all, and naturally wondered, after such words, where they stood. Robertson’s caution is just — this is not pride but the seeking of reassurance. The verse thus draws from the Lord the gracious promise to follow: that the surrender the rich ruler refused, and the disciples made, is not loss but the truest gain, abundantly repaid in this life and the next. Peter’s “we have left all” opens the door to that comfort.

So the witnesses read Peter’s word as a question more than a boast, born of the disciples’ consternation. They have just seen a rich man turn away rather than forsake his goods, and heard how hard salvation is; Peter points to what they have done — left their own to follow — and implicitly asks what shall come of it. The witnesses note the human touch: the disciples, far from wealthy, had yet given up their little all. Robertson’s caution is just — this is not pride but the seeking of reassurance, which draws from the Lord the gracious promise to follow.
29:
On Luke 18:29 — “Verily I say unto you, There is no man that hath left house, or parents, or brethren, or wife, or children, for the kingdom of God’s sake” — the witnesses mark the Lord’s gracious promise to those who forsake all.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the catalogue and its motive. Ellicott observes the omission of “lands,” found in the other Gospels — “to leave a house implied the breaking-up of… home and its relationships” (Matthew 19:29; Mark 10:29). The decisive phrase is “for the kingdom of God’s sake” (Deuteronomy 33:9); the sacrifice counts only when made for Christ, not for any lesser cause.

The verse answers Peter with a solemn assurance. No one who has forsaken the dearest ties — home, kindred, even wife and children — for the kingdom’s sake shall do so in vain; the surrender is seen and remembered by God.

So the witnesses read the promise as the Lord’s tender response to the disciples’ sacrifice. He names the closest and costliest of bonds — house, parents, brethren, wife, children — and declares that none who leaves them for his sake goes unrewarded. The witnesses mark the qualifying clause as essential: it is “for the kingdom of God’s sake” — the renunciation must be for Christ and the gospel, not for ambition, restlessness, or mere asceticism, or it earns nothing. The verse thus dignifies and reassures the self-denial the rich ruler refused: what looks like sheer loss — the breaking of home and family for the kingdom — is, in God’s reckoning, an investment he will abundantly repay. The next verse will name the return; here the Lord first sets the ground, that no sacrifice made for him is ever forgotten or unrequited.

So the witnesses read the promise as the Lord’s tender response to the disciples’ sacrifice. He names the closest and costliest of bonds — house, parents, brethren, wife, children — and declares that none who leaves them for his sake goes unrewarded. The witnesses mark the qualifying clause as essential: it is “for the kingdom of God’s sake” — the renunciation must be for Christ, not for ambition or mere asceticism, or it earns nothing. What looks like sheer loss is, in God’s reckoning, an investment he will abundantly repay; no sacrifice made for him is ever forgotten.
30:
On Luke 18:30 — “Who shall not receive manifold more in this present time, and in the world to come life everlasting” — the witnesses mark the double reward of forsaking all.

The grammatical-historical commentators read the recompense. Robertson notes the strong double negative and “manifold more” (pollaplasiona, a late word, here and Matthew 19:29). Gill takes the present reward not as more houses or kin but “comfort, peace, satisfaction… preferable to them.” Ellicott notes Luke’s characteristic omissions — the “twelve thrones,” the “with persecutions,” the “first shall be last” — keeping the promise simple. Barnes points to “life everlasting” as the crown (Revelation 2:10).

The verse completes the promise with its two-fold reward. Those who forsake all for the kingdom receive “manifold more” even now, in this present time, and beyond it life everlasting. The loss is repaid abundantly here and crowned hereafter.

So the witnesses read the Lord’s answer to every fear of loss in his service. What is given up for the kingdom is not gone but exchanged, and exchanged for vastly more. The witnesses note, with Gill, that the present “manifold more” is chiefly spiritual — the peace, joy, communion, and the wider family of faith that recompense the natural ties surrendered — not a promise of earthly wealth. And the second clause names the surpassing reward: in the world to come, life everlasting, the very thing the rich ruler sought and forfeited. The witnesses thus close the encounter with the rich ruler on a note of overflowing grace: the man who would not leave his riches lost both the present joy and the eternal life; the disciples who left their all gain both. The verse seals the lesson that nothing surrendered for Christ is lost, and that his recompense, in time and eternity, immeasurably outweighs the cost.

So the witnesses read the Lord’s answer to every fear of loss in his service. What is given up for the kingdom is not gone but exchanged, and for vastly more. The witnesses note, with Gill, that the present “manifold more” is chiefly spiritual — the peace, joy, communion, and wider family of faith — not earthly wealth. And the second clause names the surpassing reward: life everlasting, the very thing the rich ruler sought and forfeited. The man who would not leave his riches lost both present joy and eternal life; the disciples who left their all gain both. Nothing surrendered for Christ is lost.
31:
On Luke 18:31 — “Then he took unto him the twelve, and said… Behold, we go up to Jerusalem, and all things… written by the prophets concerning the Son of man shall be accomplished” — the witnesses mark the third foretelling of the Passion.

The Eastern and Latin fathers read its purpose. Gregory: foreseeing that the disciples’ hearts “would be troubled at His Passion, He tells them long beforehand both the suffering… and the glory of His Resurrection.” Chrysostom: he speaks “apart… that being habituated by expectation, they might be the more able to bear it,” as Isaiah foretold (Isaiah 53:5). Cyril of Jerusalem and Cyril stress that he “of His own accord came to it”: “the Son of man is betrayed to be crucified… lest the whole world should perish.” The grammatical-historical Robertson: “taking along with himself” the Twelve apart (Matthew 20:17), making “a special point of explaining his death.”

The verse marks the Lord’s deliberate preparation of his disciples. As he sets his face to Jerusalem, he takes the Twelve aside and tells them plainly that all the prophets wrote of the Son of man is now to be fulfilled — his suffering foreseen and foreordained.

So the witnesses read the third Passion-prediction as an act of love and proof of foreknowledge. The Lord does not stumble unawares into his death; he goes up to Jerusalem knowing all, willing all, and tells his own beforehand — that, forewarned, their faith might survive the shock. The fathers fasten on two truths: that his suffering fulfils the prophets, so that no heretic may say he taught against the law; and that he comes to it freely, the willing victim for the world’s sin, not the helpless prey of his enemies. The witnesses note, with Gregory, that he joins the glory of the Resurrection to the shame of the cross, lest the disciples despair. The verse thus opens the road to Calvary with the Lord in full command of his fate, walking knowingly toward the death that Scripture appointed and his own love embraced.

So the witnesses read the third Passion-prediction as an act of love and proof of foreknowledge. The Lord does not stumble unawares into his death; he goes up to Jerusalem knowing all, willing all, and tells his own beforehand, that their faith might survive the shock. The fathers fasten on two truths: that his suffering fulfils the prophets, so no heretic may say he taught against the law; and that he comes to it freely, the willing victim for the world’s sin. He joins the glory of the Resurrection to the shame of the cross, lest the disciples despair.
32:
On Luke 18:32 — “For he shall be delivered unto the Gentiles, and shall be mocked, and spitefully entreated, and spitted on” — the witnesses mark the catalogue of the Passion’s shame.

The voices here are brief. The grammatical-historical Ellicott notes the words “are nearly the same as in the other Gospels, but the ‘spitefully entreated’ is peculiar to St. Luke.” Gill identifies the fulfilment: delivered by the chief priests to Pilate, the Roman governor, “and by him to the soldiers” (Acts 3:13).

The verse details the indignities the Son of man will suffer. He shall be handed over to the Gentiles — the Romans — and by them mocked, insulted, and spat upon: the full measure of contempt heaped on the Lord of glory.

So the witnesses read the precise foretelling of Christ’s humiliation. He names not only his death but its shame — the delivering to the Gentiles, the mockery, the spitting — each of which the Gospels record as literally fulfilled. The witnesses note Luke’s added word, “spitefully entreated,” deepening the picture of malice. That the Messiah should be handed to the heathen and treated with such scorn ran utterly counter to the disciples’ hopes of a triumphant king, which is why, as later verses say, they could not take it in. Yet the Lord sets it all before them plainly, that when it came they might remember he had foreseen and accepted it. The verse thus underscores both the depth of his coming abasement and the exactness of his foreknowledge: every insult was named beforehand by the One who walked willingly toward it, that the cross might be seen not as defeat but as the appointed path of redemption.

So the witnesses read the precise foretelling of Christ’s humiliation. He names not only his death but its shame — the delivering to the Gentiles, the mockery, the spitting — each literally fulfilled. The witnesses note Luke’s added word, “spitefully entreated,” deepening the picture of malice. That the Messiah should be handed to the heathen and so scorned ran utterly counter to the disciples’ hopes, which is why they could not take it in. Yet the Lord sets it before them plainly, that the cross might be seen not as defeat but as the appointed path of redemption, every insult named beforehand.
33:
On Luke 18:33 — “And they shall scourge him, and put him to death: and the third day he shall rise again” — the witnesses mark the death foretold and the resurrection joined to it.

The voices here are brief. The grammatical-historical Robertson notes “the third day” (Matthew 20:19; Mark’s “after three days” in the same sense). The Wesleyan-Arminian Clarke reads the resurrection through prophecy (Hosea 6:2): “in the third day he will raise Him up… his resurrection shall be the pledge, token, and cause of ours.” Gill notes the scourging fulfilled “by Pilate’s order, before he was crucified.”

The verse completes the prediction with its darkest and its brightest word: scourging and death, then resurrection on the third day. The Lord does not leave his disciples with the cross alone but joins to it the certain rising that crowns it.

So the witnesses read the foretelling brought to its end — and its hope. The scourge and the killing are named without flinching; but the prophecy does not stop at the tomb. “The third day he shall rise again”: the very heart of the gospel is set beside the suffering, that the disciples might see the Passion as the path to glory, not the end of it. The witnesses note, with Clarke, the prophetic root in Hosea and the deep truth that Christ’s resurrection is the pledge and cause of the believer’s own. The witnesses also mark how completely the Lord controls his own narrative — scourging, death, and rising all foretold in order, that none might think him overtaken by events. The verse thus seals the third Passion-prediction with resurrection hope: the cross is real, but it is not the last word; the third day answers the dark days, and the Son of man who is put to death will rise.

So the witnesses read the foretelling brought to its end — and its hope. The scourge and the killing are named without flinching; but the prophecy does not stop at the tomb. “The third day he shall rise again”: the heart of the gospel is set beside the suffering, that the disciples might see the Passion as the path to glory, not the end of it. The witnesses mark, with Clarke, the prophetic root in Hosea and the truth that Christ’s resurrection is the pledge and cause of the believer’s own. The third day answers the dark days; the Son of man put to death will rise.
34:
On Luke 18:34 — “And they understood none of these things… and they knew not the things which were spoken” — the witnesses mark the disciples’ blindness to the plain prophecy.

The grammatical-historical commentators dwell on the threefold statement. Robertson: “they kept on not perceiving… this saying was hid from them” — Luke says it three times in one sentence. Barnes and Wesley find the cause in their fixed expectation of “a temporal prince and a conqueror”: the literal words they grasped, but could not reconcile them with their hopes. Ellicott notes the verse is “peculiar to St. Luke,” reproducing Luke 9:45, the physician dwelling on the “psychological” cause of their later bewilderment.

The verse records, with emphatic repetition, that the disciples did not understand. Plain as the Lord’s words were, their meaning was hidden from them; they could not take in that the Messiah should suffer and die.

So the witnesses read the disciples’ incomprehension as the fruit of their preconceptions. The Lord had spoken as plainly as words allow — betrayal, mockery, scourging, death, rising — yet it was “hid from them,” for it clashed utterly with their dream of a conquering Messiah and an earthly kingdom. The witnesses note Calvin’s wonder at “what stupidity” this was, and the deeper reason — that the time of full understanding was not yet, and the truth would open to them only after the events and the gift of the Spirit. They mark, too, the honesty of the record: the Evangelist does not hide the dullness of the Twelve. The verse thus prepares us for the disciples’ later dismay at the cross, and shows how a fixed false hope can blind even devoted hearts to the clearest word — a warning against reading our own expectations into the teaching of the Lord.

So the witnesses read the disciples’ incomprehension as the fruit of their preconceptions. The Lord had spoken as plainly as words allow, yet it was “hid from them,” for it clashed with their dream of a conquering Messiah. The witnesses note Calvin’s wonder at “what stupidity” this was, and the deeper reason — that the time of full understanding was not yet, and the truth would open only after the events and the Spirit. They mark the honesty of the record, which does not hide the dullness of the Twelve — a warning against reading our own expectations into the word of the Lord.
35:
On Luke 18:35 — “And it came to pass, that as he was come nigh unto Jericho, a certain blind man sat by the way side begging” — the witnesses mark the healing of the blind man at Jericho.

The Eastern and Latin fathers read it typologically. Gregory: the disciples being “yet carnal… unable to receive the words of mystery… are brought to a miracle… that… their faith might be strengthened.” Theophylact: the Lord “did not even walk without doing good.” Ambrose: “in the blind man we have a type of the Gentile people, who have received… the brightness of the light which they had lost.” The grammatical-historical commentators address the harmony: Robertson on the two Jerichos (old and new Roman), and that Mark names him Bartimaeus (Mark 10:46) while Matthew mentions two (Matthew 20:30).

The verse opens the last miracle before Jerusalem. As the Lord nears Jericho on his way to the cross, a blind beggar sits by the road — helpless, destitute, in darkness — about to become the occasion of mercy and a sign of faith.

So the witnesses set the healing as a deliberate counterpoint to the disciples’ blindness just confessed. Where the Twelve could not see the meaning of the Passion, a blind man by the way will see and confess the Son of David; the contrast, the fathers note, is pointed. The witnesses address the apparent discrepancy among the Gospels — one blind man or two, at the entering or the leaving of Jericho — explaining it by the two Jerichos and the Evangelists’ selective focus, without doubting the event. And they draw out the type: the blind beggar stands for the Gentiles, sitting in darkness, who receive their sight from Christ as he passes. The verse thus begins a story of faith that sees where sight is lacking, set against carnal eyes that are blind amid full light — and shows the Lord, even on the road to his own suffering, pausing to give light to one who cried for mercy.

So the witnesses set the healing as a deliberate counterpoint to the disciples’ blindness just confessed. Where the Twelve could not see the meaning of the Passion, a blind man by the way will see and confess the Son of David. The witnesses address the apparent discrepancy among the Gospels — one blind man or two, entering or leaving Jericho — explaining it by the two Jerichos and selective focus, without doubting the event. And they draw out the type: the blind beggar stands for the Gentiles, sitting in darkness, who receive their sight from Christ as he passes.
36:
On Luke 18:36 — “And hearing the multitude pass by, he asked what it meant” — the witnesses mark the blind man’s first stirring toward Christ.

The voices here are brief. The grammatical-historical commentators read the detail. Ellicott: better “a multitude,” the Greek without the article, “better expressing the vague impression left on the blind man by the sound of many footsteps and voices.” Robertson notes the imperfect “inquired” — “repeatedly inquired as he heard the tramp of the passing crowd.”

The verse catches the beggar at the threshold of his opportunity. Unable to see, he hears the stir of a passing throng and, eager and persistent, keeps asking what it means — the first motion of a soul reaching toward the light it cannot yet behold.

So the witnesses find significance in the blind man’s alert inquiry. He cannot see, but he hears; and rather than sit passive, he asks, and keeps asking, what the commotion is. The witnesses note the spiritual fitness of the picture — the soul in darkness, stirred by the report of Christ’s nearness, pressing to learn more. The vague sound of the crowd is all he has, yet he does not let the moment pass; his repeated questioning is itself a kind of seeking. As Pseudo-Chrysostom observed, he “grasped with his heart what his sight embraced not.” The verse thus shows faith’s beginning in one who uses what little he has — his hearing, his persistence — to find the Saviour. The contrast with the seeing crowd, who merely pass by, is plain: the blind beggar’s eager inquiry will lead him to the sight the sighted lacked.

So the witnesses find significance in the blind man’s alert inquiry. He cannot see, but he hears; and rather than sit passive, he asks, and keeps asking, what the commotion is. The witnesses note the spiritual fitness — the soul in darkness, stirred by the report of Christ’s nearness, pressing to learn more. The vague sound of the crowd is all he has, yet he does not let the moment pass. As Pseudo-Chrysostom observed, he “grasped with his heart what his sight embraced not.” His eager inquiry, unlike the seeing crowd who merely pass by, will lead him to the sight the sighted lacked.
37:
On Luke 18:37 — “And they told him, that Jesus of Nazareth passeth by” — the witnesses mark the answer that meets the blind man’s seeking.

The voices here are brief. The grammatical-historical Robertson notes the present “passeth by,” retained in the report. The Reformed Gill fills out the scene: some of the company answered that “Jesus, the prophet of Nazareth, so much talked of, and said to be the Messiah… famous for his doctrines and miracles… was just then passing by.”

The verse gives the crowd’s reply to the beggar’s question. They tell him it is Jesus of Nazareth who is passing — the name of the prophet whose fame for teaching and healing had spread through the land. The moment of opportunity is named.

So the witnesses mark the providence in the answer. The blind man’s persistent asking is met with the one piece of knowledge he needs: that Jesus of Nazareth is at hand. The witnesses note that the crowd speaks of him by his common, even lowly, title — “of Nazareth” — the name of his reproach; yet the beggar, as the next verse shows, will rise above their estimate and hail him “Son of David.” The report is enough to kindle his faith into action; what he has heard of Jesus’s mighty works now becomes a present hope, for the worker is passing within reach. The verse thus shows how God brings the seeking soul the word it needs at the very moment it can act — and how the same announcement that the crowd makes carelessly becomes, to a heart prepared, the call to salvation. The beggar will not let “Jesus of Nazareth” pass him by.

So the witnesses mark the providence in the answer. The blind man’s persistent asking is met with the one thing he needs: that Jesus of Nazareth is at hand. The witnesses note that the crowd speaks of him by his lowly title — “of Nazareth,” the name of his reproach — yet the beggar will rise above their estimate and hail him “Son of David.” The report is enough to kindle his faith into action; the same announcement the crowd makes carelessly becomes, to a heart prepared, the call to salvation. He will not let “Jesus of Nazareth” pass him by.
38:
On Luke 18:38 — “And he cried, saying, Jesus, thou Son of David, have mercy on me” — the witnesses mark the blind man’s confession of faith.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the cry and its title. Robertson: “Son of David” (huie Daueid) “shows that he recognizes Jesus as the Messiah” (Matthew 9:27); “he cried” (eboesen), shouted aloud. Gill: “believing him at once to be the Messiah,” he calls him “Son of David,” a known title of the Christ. The Eastern Cyril marks that, “brought up a Jew,” he knew “of the seed of David should God be born” — confessing him both God and Son of David.

The verse gives the beggar’s great cry. Hearing it is Jesus, he shouts above the crowd, hailing him not “of Nazareth” but “Son of David” — the Messianic title — and begs for mercy. His blindness has not hindered his sight of who Jesus is.

So the witnesses read in the blind man’s cry a clearer vision than the seeing crowd possessed. They named Jesus by his earthly town; he names him by his royal, Messianic descent — “Son of David” — confessing the very truth the disciples could not yet grasp. The witnesses note the boldness and the humility together: he cries aloud, unashamed, yet pleads only for mercy, claiming no merit. Cyril marvels that this untaught beggar held what learned men divided — calling Christ both God and the seed of David. The verse thus crowns the contrast of the chapter: the physically blind man sees Christ truly, while those with eyes are blind to him; and his faith, like the publican’s, casts itself wholly on mercy. His “have mercy on me” is the cry that the whole chapter has commended — persistent, humble, believing — and it will not go unanswered.

So the witnesses read in the blind man’s cry a clearer vision than the seeing crowd possessed. They named Jesus by his earthly town; he names him by his royal, Messianic descent — confessing the very truth the disciples could not grasp. The witnesses note the boldness and humility together: he cries aloud, unashamed, yet pleads only for mercy. Cyril marvels that this untaught beggar held what learned men divided, calling Christ both God and seed of David. His “have mercy on me” is the cry the whole chapter has commended — persistent, humble, believing — and it will not go unanswered.
39:
On Luke 18:39 — “And they which went before rebuked him, that he should hold his peace: but he cried so much the more” — the witnesses mark the opposition overcome by importunity.

The voices here are brief. The grammatical-historical commentators read the crowd and the cry. Ellicott: “they which went before” were “in advance of Jesus… probably… a portion of the crowd,” not the disciples. Robertson: “that he should become silent… the more a great deal” (Mark 10:48) — the rebuke only redoubled his cry. Barnes points to the like persistence (Psalm 141:1).

The verse shows the beggar’s faith tested and proved by opposition. The crowd, going before, tries to silence him; but rather than be hushed, he cries all the louder, refusing to lose his one chance of mercy.

So the witnesses find in the blind man’s persistence the very lesson of the chapter’s opening parable. Like the importunate widow, he will not be put off; the more he is told to be quiet, the more he shouts “Son of David, have mercy.” The witnesses note that the hindrance came from the crowd around Christ — those who should have helped him to the Lord instead tried to stop him — yet their rebuke only strengthened his resolve. His faith was of the kind that obstacles cannot quench; he knew his need too well and his opportunity too precious to be silenced. The verse thus joins the persistent prayer of verse 1 to the persistent cry of verse 39: the man who keeps crying, against discouragement, is the man who is heard. The beggar’s redoubled shout, in the face of the crowd’s effort to hush him, is the importunity that prevails — and the Lord, in the next verse, stands still.

So the witnesses find in the blind man’s persistence the very lesson of the chapter’s opening parable. Like the importunate widow, he will not be put off; the more he is told to be quiet, the more he shouts. The witnesses note that the hindrance came from the crowd around Christ — those who should have helped him instead tried to stop him — yet their rebuke only strengthened his resolve. His faith was of the kind obstacles cannot quench; the man who keeps crying, against discouragement, is the man who is heard, and the Lord, in the next verse, stands still.
40:
On Luke 18:40 — “And Jesus stood, and commanded him to be brought unto him” — the witnesses mark the Lord halting for the beggar.

The voices here are brief. The grammatical-historical Robertson: “stood” — Jesus “stopped” (Mark 10:49); “be brought” a first aorist infinitive in indirect command. Gill: “he stood still; he made a full stop… when… he was just over against him.”

The verse marks the Lord’s response to the persistent cry. On his way to Jerusalem and the cross, he halts the whole procession and commands that the blind beggar be brought to him. The importunity that the crowd would silence, Christ honours by stopping for it.

So the witnesses see in the Lord’s standing still a touching proof of his attentiveness to the crying soul. Bound for his own great suffering, surrounded by a throng, he yet stops for one blind beggar whom others tried to hush. The witnesses note the reversal: the crowd had pushed the man aside, but the Lord commands him to be brought near, overruling their rebuke with his welcome. The very persistence that annoyed the bystanders is what the Lord rewards; he will not pass by the faith that calls on him. The verse thus answers the importunate widow’s parable with a living instance: the cry that would not be silenced has reached the ear of Christ, and he stops — the Son of David standing still at the call of a beggar. It teaches that no genuine, persistent appeal to Christ is ever beneath his notice, however the world may try to brush it away.

So the witnesses see in the Lord’s standing still a touching proof of his attentiveness to the crying soul. Bound for his own great suffering, surrounded by a throng, he yet stops for one blind beggar whom others tried to hush. The witnesses note the reversal: the crowd had pushed the man aside, but the Lord commands him brought near, overruling their rebuke with his welcome. The very persistence that annoyed the bystanders is what he rewards. No genuine, persistent appeal to Christ is ever beneath his notice, however the world may try to brush it away.
41:
On Luke 18:41 — “What wilt thou that I shall do unto thee? And he said, Lord, that I may receive my sight” — the witnesses mark the question and the definite request.

The voices here are brief. The grammatical-historical Ellicott notes that Luke’s “Lord” (kyrie) stands for Mark’s “Rabboni,” perhaps “in a somewhat higher sense.” Robertson notes the idiom and the threefold use of “receive sight” (Mark 10:51). Gill: “is it alms thou askest? or… thy sight… restored?”

The verse records the Lord’s gracious question and the beggar’s clear answer. Christ, who knew his need, asks what he would have — drawing out his faith into definite request; and the man, no longer begging coins, asks the great thing: that he may see.

So the witnesses find meaning in the Lord’s asking what he already knew. He bids the blind man name his desire, that his faith may be expressed and his request made his own. The witnesses note the advance in the beggar’s asking: by the roadside he had sought alms, but now, brought to Christ, he seeks his sight — the larger mercy that only the Son of David can give. His answer is direct and believing: “Lord, that I may receive my sight.” The witnesses mark Ellicott’s note on his address — “Lord,” a word of higher reverence than mere “Master” — matching the confession “Son of David.” The verse thus shows faith brought to the point of definite petition: Christ draws the request from the heart, and the man, asking not for the lesser but the greatest thing in his power to receive, lays before the Lord the very desire that Christ stands ready to fulfil.

So the witnesses find meaning in the Lord’s asking what he already knew. He bids the blind man name his desire, that his faith may be expressed and his request made his own. The witnesses note the advance in his asking: by the roadside he sought alms, but now, brought to Christ, he seeks his sight — the larger mercy only the Son of David can give. His answer is direct and believing; and his address, “Lord,” a word of higher reverence, matches his confession “Son of David.” Christ draws the request from the heart, laying before himself the very desire he stands ready to fulfil.
42:
On Luke 18:42 — “And Jesus said unto him, Receive thy sight: thy faith hath saved thee” — the witnesses mark the word that heals and the faith that received it.

The voices here are brief but weighty. The grammatical-historical Ellicott: better, as in Mark, “thy faith hath made thee whole” — the immediate reference to his sight, but, as in Luke 7:50, the word carrying “a deeper meaning” of salvation. Gill: by “a word speaking, and power going along with it, without… any means, he fulfilled his desire” (Luke 17:19).

The verse gives the cure in a word and names its instrument. Christ speaks, and sight is restored; and he ascribes the blessing to the man’s faith — “thy faith hath saved thee” — the persistent, believing cry that brought him to the Lord.

So the witnesses read in the healing both the Lord’s power and the beggar’s faith honoured. By a bare word, without touch or means, Christ opens the blind eyes — a sovereign act of power. Yet he credits the man’s faith: “thy faith hath saved thee.” The witnesses note the double sense Ellicott marks — the word means both “made whole” in body and “saved” in soul, the bodily healing a sign of the deeper salvation faith obtains. The faith commended is the very faith the chapter has traced: persistent against discouragement, humble in pleading mercy, confessing Christ as Son of David. The verse thus gathers the chapter’s teaching into one act: the importunate, believing soul that cries to Christ for mercy receives both sight and salvation. What the rich ruler forfeited by self-trust, the blind beggar gains by faith; and the Lord, on his road to the cross, gives light and life to one who would not be silenced.

So the witnesses read in the healing both the Lord’s power and the beggar’s faith honoured. By a bare word, without touch or means, Christ opens the blind eyes — a sovereign act of power. Yet he credits the man’s faith. The witnesses note the double sense Ellicott marks — “made whole” in body and “saved” in soul, the healing a sign of the deeper salvation. What the rich ruler forfeited by self-trust, the blind beggar gains by faith; and the Lord, on his road to the cross, gives light and life to one who would not be silenced.
43:
On Luke 18:43 — “And immediately he received his sight, and followed him, glorifying God: and all the people… gave praise unto God” — the witnesses mark the instant cure and its fruit.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the immediacy and the response. Gill: “as soon as ever the word was gone out… virtue went forth… his eyes were at once opened.” Robertson: “followed” (imperfect) — “he began to follow” (Mark 10:52). Ellicott: the effect “on the blind man himself, and on the people, is peculiar to St. Luke,” who loved to record such glorifying of God (Luke 5:26; Acts 3:8). Clarke notes “the common people are often better judges of the work of God than the doctors.”

The verse completes the miracle with its threefold fruit: the man sees at once, follows Jesus, and glorifies God; and the watching crowd joins in praise. The cure issues in discipleship and worship.

So the witnesses read the healing’s outcome as the pattern of true conversion. The sight is given instantly, by the bare word of Christ; and the healed man does not go his own way but follows Jesus — the very road that leads to Jerusalem and the cross — glorifying God as he goes. The witnesses note that his gratitude becomes discipleship: he uses his new sight to follow the Giver. And the joy spreads — the whole people, seeing the work, praise God, the simple crowd discerning, as Clarke says, what the proud doctors would not. The verse thus closes the chapter on a note of light, worship, and following, in sharp contrast to its earlier figures: where the rich ruler went away sorrowful and the disciples walked in darkness, the once-blind beggar walks after Christ rejoicing. It is the fitting end of a chapter on humble, persistent faith — sight received, the Saviour followed, and God glorified.

The sight is given instantly, by the bare word of Christ; and the healed man does not go his own way but follows Jesus — the very road to Jerusalem and the cross — glorifying God.
